body:
# Prisma + Postgres Workflow Skill

A Claude Code skill that knows the current Prisma workflow deeply. Written for Prisma 7.x + PostgreSQL 15+.

## Install

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.claude/skills/prisma-postgres/references
# drop SKILL.md and references/ into that directory
```

## SKILL.md

```markdown
---
name: prisma-postgres
description: >
  Use when editing a Prisma schema, writing migrations, debugging connection or
  query errors, or auditing for N+1 patterns. Triggers on "migration failing",
  "prisma generate not working", "connection pool", "shadow database", "P2002",
  "P2025", "prisma push", "prisma migrate dev", "seed the db", "rollback the
  migration". Skip for conceptual questions about ORMs or general SQL tutorials.
---

# Prisma + Postgres

Prisma is the ORM. PostgreSQL is the database. The friction is in the seam.

## The 5-phase workflow

### Phase 1 — Before editing the schema

Read `references/schema-rules.md`.

Always:
- Check there are no pending migrations: `npx prisma migrate status`
- Check the current Postgres version supports any new feature you're using
- Work on a branch — migrations are commits, not hot edits

### Phase 2 — Editing the schema

Read `references/common-edits.md`.

The three safe classes of edit:
- Additive: new model, new field (nullable or with default), new index
- Renaming: use @map to preserve the DB name, OR write a migration explicitly
- Non-additive: dropping columns, changing types, NOT NULL on existing column — these need a multi-step migration

### Phase 3 — Generating the migration

Read `references/migrations.md`.

```bash
npx prisma migrate dev --name <concise_name>
```

This creates a new migration folder, runs it against the shadow database (Prisma's way of verifying the migration is reversible), and applies it to the main DB.

If it fails with shadow database errors, that's Prisma saying your migration can't run cleanly — investigate, don't just `--create-only`.

### Phase 4 — Validating the result

```bash
npx prisma migrate status
npx prisma generate
npx prisma validate
```

Then write a test that exercises the new schema. No migration ships without a corresponding test.

### Phase 5 — Deploying the migration

Read `references/deploy.md`.

- `prisma migrate deploy` in CI or on the server (NOT `migrate dev`)
- Never `prisma db push` in production — it skips migration tracking
- Always have a rollback plan — the `down` migration (Prisma doesn't generate these; write them yourself for complex changes)

## Common errors this skill recognizes

| Error code | Meaning | First fix |
|---|---|---|
| P2002 | Unique constraint violation | Check the input, use upsert or handle duplicates |
| P2025 | Record not found (for update/delete) | Add a findUnique check first, or use upsert |
| P1001 | Can't reach database | DATABASE_URL issue — check host, port, TLS, firewall |
| P1008 | Timeout | Query is too slow — add an index or paginate |
| P1017 | Server closed the connection | Connection pool issue — check max pool size |
| P3006 | Migration failed to apply | Look at the shadow database error |
| P3018 | Migration failed cleanly but data is inconsistent | You need a manual data migration step |

## Connection pooling (the #1 production gotcha)

- Prisma opens one connection per instance by default
- In serverless, every function invocation can spawn a new instance → pool exhaustion
- Fix: use PgBouncer OR Prisma Data Proxy OR set `connection_limit=1` and use a pooler upstream
- For Vercel + Neon/Supabase/Railway: use the pooled connection string, not the direct one
- Add `?pgbouncer=true` to the connection string when using PgBouncer (disables prepared statements)

## N+1 audit

Common pattern:
```ts
const users = await db.user.findMany();
for (const u of users) {
  const posts = await db.post.findMany({ where: { authorId: u.id } }); // N+1!
}
```

Fix:
```ts
const users = await db.user.findMany({ include: { posts: true } });
```

Or for more complex cases, use `findMany` on the related table with an `in` filter.
```

## references/schema-rules.md (excerpt)

```
- Every model needs a primary key
- Prefer @default(cuid()) over auto-increment for distributed systems
- Use @updatedAt on every mutable model — it's almost free and saves hours in debugging
- Never use @unique on columns that can be null unless you understand the semantics
- Composite indexes: order matters, put the most-selective column first
- Add @map to every field whose Prisma name differs from the DB column
```

## references/migrations.md (excerpt)

```
# Adding a NOT NULL column to an existing table with data

This is a 3-step migration:

1. Migration 1: add the column as NULLABLE with a default
2. Application update: start writing the column in all code paths
3. Migration 2: make it NOT NULL and optionally drop the default

Never skip the intermediate nullable step — the migration will fail on existing rows.
```
